ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general fried rice class (FOODON_00002694); no specific egg fried rice subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKW covers fried rice as a category; no egg-specific fried rice code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for egg fried rice identified in the clinical terminology. Ontology gap noted.

serving.natural_language_portion: One large sharing plate of egg fried rice intended for 4 to 5 people, approximately 800 g total, equivalent to roughly 160–200 g per person.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Rice, Egg — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Fried rice with egg (composite reference)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary reference: HPB Singapore Foodo entry for egg fried rice (plain, Chinese-style). Cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy item 'Rice, fried, Chinese restaurant' (NDB 35188) and USDA entry for egg fried rice components. Values represent a standard Chinese-style egg fried rice prepared with cooked white rice, whole eggs, vegetable oil, soy sauce, spring onions, and white pepper. The large (L) portion is a restaurant sharing size; per-100g values are derived from the standard recipe composition.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Fried rice (Chinese-style, egg fried rice analogue)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for egg fried rice was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 54 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: white rice fried with egg and oil (Chinese restaurant style), referenced in Atkinson FS et al. (2021) International tables of GI and GL values, Diabetes Care. Fried rice with egg and oil has a lower GI than plain boiled white rice (GI ~72) due to the fat and protein content slowing gastric emptying and glucose absorption. GL per serving calculated as: (54 × 25.5 g carbohydrate per 100g × 160 g individual serving) / 100 ≈ 22 g. gi_category set to 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g) from soy sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size of egg fried rice to approximately 150 g per meal, as a 200 g serving provides approximately 760 mg sodium, representing over one-third of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~54) and significant carbohydrate load from white rice
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict egg fried rice to a small portion (approximately 100–150 g) per meal and pair it with non-starchy vegetables and lean protein to moderate the postprandial glycemic response, given the moderate GI and high carbohydrate density of white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~1.0 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium from soy sauce seasoning
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume egg fried rice in moderation, limiting portions to 150 g or less per meal, and request reduced soy sauce to lower sodium intake, as the combined sodium and saturated fat content may contribute to cardiovascular risk when consumed frequently.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Egg fried rice (蛋炒饭) is a classic Chinese staple dish made by stir-frying pre-cooked white rice in a hot wok with beaten whole eggs, vegetable oil, soy sauce, spring onions, salt, and white pepper. The high heat of the wok imparts a characteristic smoky 'wok hei' flavour and produces a lightly golden, slightly crispy texture on the rice grains. The eggs coat and bind the rice, adding richness and a soft, savoury flavour. This dish is ubiquitous across Chinese restaurants in Singapore and throughout Southeast Asia, served as a standalone meal or as a shared side dish. The large (L) portion from The Seafood Market is designed for 4–5 people sharing. Nutritionally, egg fried rice is a moderate-energy dish providing approximately 163 kcal per 100 g, with carbohydrates as the dominant macronutrient (~25.5 g per 100 g) from white rice, supplemented by protein (~4.5 g) from eggs and moderate fat (~4.8 g) from cooking oil and eggs. Sodium content is elevated (~380 mg per 100 g) due to soy sauce seasoning, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 54 (low category), lower than plain boiled white rice due to the fat and protein content slowing glucose absorption. This dish is suitable for vegetarians and is generally halal-compliant when prepared without pork-derived ingredients.
common_user_queries: How many calories in egg fried rice?
What is the GI of egg fried rice?
Can diabetics eat egg fried rice?
Is egg fried rice high in sodium?
How much protein is in egg fried rice?

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo entry for this specific restaurant's large egg fried rice was available. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo egg fried rice reference data and cross-validated against USDA SR Legacy fried rice with egg entries. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: 1. This record covers the large (L) sharing portion of egg fried rice from The Seafood Market (By House of White Bee Hoon) at The Riverwalk, Singapore, priced at SGD 26.00 for 4–5 pax. 2. Per-100g nutrient values are derived from HPB Singapore Foodo egg fried rice reference and USDA SR Legacy fried rice with egg (NDB 35188), adapted for a standard Chinese-style preparation. 3. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the primary HPB or USDA SR Legacy source for this food category. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the primary HPB source for egg fried rice. 5. Trans fat is 0.0 (confirmed zero) — standard Chinese-style egg fried rice prepared with vegetable oil contains negligible trans fat per HPB and USDA reference data. 6. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for egg fried rice was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. The estimate is based on the closest analogous fried rice with egg entry from Atkinson et al. (2021). 7. Serving size of 800 g total is estimated for a 4–5 pax sharing portion at SGD 26.00; individual portion is approximately 160–200 g. GL per individual serving (160 g) = (54 × 25.5 × 160) / 10000 ≈ 22 g. 8. Halal status is assumed for this restaurant context but not independently verified; kosher status is not applicable. 9. The food_id GD003572 is retained from the input seed record.
